volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Curculio (Section 1.1.74b - Section 1.2.132)

sections.label: Section 1.1.74b
sections.translation: Then you would have Venus to be sick outright .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.translation: Here , boy , give me the bowl .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75b
sections.translation: What are you going to do ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.75c
sections.translation: You ’ ll know directly . An old hag is in the habit of sleeping here , as the keeper of the door ; the name of the Procuress is Much-bibber and Neat-bibber .

sections.label: Section 1.1.78
sections.original: Quasi tu lagoenam dicas , ubi vinum Chium
sections.translation: Just as you would speak of a flagon , in which Chian wine [*] is wont to be .

sections.label: Section 1.1.79b
sections.translation: What need is there of words ? She is a most vinous soul ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: eaque extemplo ubi ego vino has conspersi fores ,
sections.translation: and the very moment that I ’ ve sprinkled this door with the wine , she knows by the smell that I ’ m here , and opens it forthwith .

sections.label: Section 1.1.82
sections.original: Eine hic cum vino sinus fertur ?
sections.translation: Is it for her the bowl is brought with the wine ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.82b
sections.translation: Unless you object .

sections.label: Section 1.1.83
sections.original: Nolo hercle , nam istunc qui fert afflictum velim ;
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I do object ; for I ’ d rather have it broken about him who has brought it . I fancied it was brought for ourselves .

sections.label: Section 1.1.84b
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you hold your tongue ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: si quid super illi fuerit , id nobis sat est .
sections.translation: If any ’ s left after her , it will be enough for ourselves .

sections.label: Section 1.1.86
sections.original: Quisnam istic fluviust , quem non recipiat mare ?
sections.translation: What river is it , pray , that the sea does not receive ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.87
sections.original: Sequere hac , Palinure , me ad fores , fi mi obsequens .
sections.translation: Follow me this way , Palinurus , to the door ; do you be obedient to me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.88
sections.translation: I ’ ll do so .

sections.label: Section 1.1.88b
sections.translation: Come , drink , you joyous door , quaff on , readily prove propitious unto me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: Voltisne olivas aut pulpamentum aut capparim ?
sections.translation: Door , would you like some olives or a tit-bit , or some capers ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.91
sections.translation: Arouse and send out here to me your portress .

sections.label: Section 1.1.92
sections.original: Profundis vinum : quae te res agitant ?
sections.translation: You ’ re spilling the wine ; what matter is it that possesses you ? ( Takes hold of his arm .)

sections.label: Section 1.1.92b
sections.translation: Let me alone . Don ’ t you see ? This most joyous door is opening ; does the hinge make a bit of creaking ? ’ Tis a charming one .

sections.label: Section 1.1.94b
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you then give it a kiss ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.translation: Hold your tongue ; let ’ s keep back the light and our noise .

sections.label: Section 1.1.95b
sections.translation: Be it so .

sections.label: Section 1.2.96
sections.original: Flós veteris vini meis náribus obiectust ,
sections.translation: The savour of aged wine has reached my nostrils ; the love of it has brought me in my eagerness hither in the dark ; wherever it is , it ’ s near me . O capital , I ’ ve found it . ( Stooping , and smelling at the bowl .) Hail to you , my soul , Joy of dear Bacchus ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: ut véteris vetus tui cúpida sum .
sections.translation: how enamoured am I of your old age . For in comparison with yours , the odour of all unguents were mere bilge-water ; you are my myrrh , you my cinnamon , you my rose , you my saffron unguent and my cassia , you are my vine-palm [*] scent . But , where you have been poured , there would I most earnestly hope to be buried .

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: séd quom adhuc náso odos óbsecutúst meo ,
sections.translation: But since as yet you , fragrance , alone have penetrated to my nostrils , so in its turn afford some delight to my throat . ( Feels for the bowl on the ground , which PALINURUS draws away .) I find you not ; where is your own dear self ? I ’ m longing to touch you ; do let me pour your liquids into me by sip-sipping . But in this direction it has gone , this way I ’ ll . follow it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.translation: This old lady ’ s thirsty ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.110b
sections.translation: how limited is her thirst ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.110c
sections.translation: She ’ s a moderate person ; she swallows eight gallons [*] only .

sections.label: Section 1.2.111
sections.original: Pol ut praédicas , vindémia haec huic ánui non sat est sóli .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , according to your account , this year ’ s vintage is not enough for this old woman alone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.112
sections.original: canem esse hánc quidem magis pár fuit : sagax násum habet .
sections.translation: It really had been more proper for her to be a dog ; she has a good nose .

sections.label: Section 1.2.112b
sections.translation: Prithee , whose voice is it that I hear at a distance .

sections.label: Section 1.2.113
sections.translation: I think that this old hag should be accosted . I ’ ll approach her . ( Aloud .) Come back , Procuress , and look back towards me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.translation: Who is it that gives his commands ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.116
sections.translation: The all-powerful in wine , jolly Bacchus ; he who , when you ’ re hawking , parched , and half asleep , brings you a draught and comes to quench your thirst .

sections.label: Section 1.2.119
sections.translation: How far is he away from me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.119b
sections.translation: See this light .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: Grándiorém gradum ergó fac ad me , óbsecro .
sections.translation: Then , prithee , do quicken your pace towards me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121
sections.translation: Health to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121b
sections.original: Egon sálva sim , quaé siti sícca sum ?
sections.translation: How can I have health , who am parched with thirst ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.121c
sections.translation: But you shall drink in a moment .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121d
sections.translation: ’ Tis long a-coming .

sections.label: Section 1.2.122
sections.translation: Here ’ s for you , jolly old dame .

sections.label: Section 1.2.122b
sections.translation: Health to you , gentleman dear as my very eyes .

sections.label: Section 1.2.123
sections.original: Age , effúnde hoc cito in barathrúm , propere
sections.translation: Come , toss this off quickly into your abyss ; scour out your sink right speedily .

sections.label: Section 1.2.124b
sections.translation: Hold your tongue ; I won ’ t have her ill spoken to .

sections.label: Section 1.2.124c
sections.translation: In preference , then , I ’ ll do her ill .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: Venús , de paulo paúlulum hoc tíbi dabo haud lubénter .
sections.translation: Venus , of this little , this very little will I give to thee [*] — sore against my will ; for all the lovers , in their cups , to propitiate thee , expend their wine upon thee : not such windfalls often fall to me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.128
sections.original: Hoc víde ut ingurgitat ímpura in se mérum avariter , faucíbus plenis .
sections.translation: Do look at that , please , how greedily the filthy hag swills down the pure wine into herself with distended gullet .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129
sections.original: Perii hércle , huic quid primúm dicam nesció .
sections.translation: Troth now , I ’ m quite undone ; what first to say to her I know not .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129b
sections.translation: Why , the same thing that you said to me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.translation: What ’ s that ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.130b
sections.translation: Say that you ’ re quite undone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130c
sections.translation: May the Gods confound you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130d
sections.translation: Say so to her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.131
sections.translation: Ha , ha , ha

sections.label: Section 1.2.131b
sections.translation: How is it ? Do you like it ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.131c
sections.translation: I do like it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.131d
sections.original: Etiam mihi quóque stimulo foderé lubet te .
sections.translation: And I , too , should like to goad with a spur as well .

sections.label: Section 1.2.132
sections.translation: Don ’ t you — do hold your tongue .
